mission.title: Install and Configure Karmada on Kubernetes
mission.description: Karmada is an open-source multi-cloud, multi-cluster Kubernetes orchestration tool that enables seamless management of cloud-native applications across various Kubernetes clusters. Installing Karmada allows you to leverage its advanced scheduling capabilities and centralized management for improved application deployment and scaling.

steps.title: Add the Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the Karmada Helm chart repository to your local Helm client with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add karmada https://charts.karmada.io
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Karmada using Helm
steps.description:
Install Karmada with the following command, creating a namespace called 'karmada-system':
```bash
helm install karmada-operator karmada/karmada-operator --namespace karmada-system --create-namespace --version v1.17.0
```

steps.title: Verify the installation
steps.description:
Check if the Karmada operator pods are running:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n karmada-system
```

steps.title: Configure Resource Limits
steps.description:
Edit the Karmada operator configuration to set resource limits. You can do this by updating the values.yaml file or using `kubectl`:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment karmada-operator -n karmada-system
# Add resource limits under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     cpu: 500m
#     memory: 512Mi
```

steps.title: Set up RBAC
steps.description:
Ensure that the necessary RBAC permissions are set for Karmada to function correctly:
```bash
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/karmada-io/karmada/master/docs/install/rbac.yaml
```

steps.title: Access Karmada Dashboard
steps.description:
If Karmada provides a dashboard, you can access it using port-forwarding:
```bash
kubectl port-forward service/karmada-dashboard -n karmada-system 8080:80
# Now access the dashboard at http://localhost:8080
```

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Karmada operator using Helm:
```bash
helm uninstall karmada-operator --namespace karmada-system
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs
uninstall.description:
Remove any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) that were installed:
```bash
kubectl delete crd $(kubectl get crd | grep karmada | awk '{print $1}')
```

uninstall.title: Remove the namespace
uninstall.description:
Delete the Karmada namespace to clean up any remaining resources:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace karmada-system
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current configuration and resources:
```bash
kubectl get all -n karmada-system -o yaml > karmada-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update the Helm repository
upgrade.description:
Ensure your Helm repository is up to date:
```bash
helm repo update
```

upgrade.title: Upgrade Karmada
upgrade.description:
Upgrade to the latest version of Karmada:
```bash
helm upgrade karmada-operator karmada/karmada-operator --namespace karmada-system --version v1.17.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check the status of the Karmada operator pods to ensure they are running:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n karmada-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Pods stuck in CrashLoopBackOff
troubleshooting.description:
If the Karmada operator pods are in a CrashLoopBackOff state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs <pod-name> -n karmada-system
# Replace <pod-name> with the actual name of the pod
```

troubleshooting.title: Karmada services not reachable
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot reach Karmada services, ensure the services are running:
```bash
kubectl get svc -n karmada-system
```

troubleshooting.title: RBAC permissions issues
troubleshooting.description:
If you encounter permission errors, verify that the RBAC configuration is correctly applied:
```bash
kubectl get clusterrolebindings | grep karmada
```

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing OOMKilled
troubleshooting.description:
If pods are being killed due to OOM (Out Of Memory), consider increasing the memory limits in the deployment:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment karmada-operator -n karmada-system
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Karmada will result in the Karmada operator running in the 'karmada-system' namespace with all necessary pods in a 'Running' state. You can access the Karmada dashboard if configured.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add karmada https://charts.karmada.io
helm repo update
helm install karmada-operator karmada/karmada-operator --namespace karmada-system --create-namespace --version v1.17.0

kubectl edit deployment karmada-operator -n karmada-system
# Add resource limits under spec.containers:
# resources:
#   limits:
#     cpu: 500m
#     memory: 512Mi

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have Helm and kubectl installed and configured to interact with your Kubernetes cluster.
